title: Deloitte's $1b back-office bet as AI threatens jobs
full_text:
Deloitte Australia wants to more than triple its managed services arm into a $1 billion business by 2030, offsetting the threat from artificial intelligence that may automate one-third of routine consulting tasks within three years.
Joanne Gorton, Deloitte Australia's chief executive, said AI would allow the firm to operate back-office functions, such as finance, more efficiently for clients than they can do for themselves.
Gorton said clients should share this "AI dividend" with the firm, but admits the result may be a lowering of fees for some services, possibly by as much as 40 per cent.
Part of her client pitch is that Deloitte was investing heavily in technology and training, and absorbing the operational risk of running back-office functions for clients.
The strategy reflects the pivot being undertaken by the big four consulting firms to adapt as smaller firms and technology, such as AI, gut the traditional "time and motion" billing model for advisory work.
Major consulting firms are now locked in a global AI arms race, pumping tens of billions at the global level into software to replace automated services. They are also now shifting to "value" pricing, which is based on the benefits received by the client rather than by time spent on work, to justify premium fees.
Deloitte's strategy follows six months of work that included a study with the University of Sydney into what advisory tasks AI would likely automate. It also asked clients what they wanted from the firm in the coming years, and tapped into internal and external research about the future of the profession.
The research revealed that AI may automate 30 per cent of routine consulting tasks within three years.
Still, the firm's in-house forecasters, Deloitte Access Economics, projected that the consulting sector would grow faster than the economy overall, at 8 per cent a year. This was more bullish than the 5 per cent growth estimated by Source Global Research.
It remains unclear who will capture that growth. Will it be Deloitte and its big rivals, as well as tech consulting giant Accenture? Or will it be nimbler rivals such as consulting and insolvency firm Alvarez & Marsal, or AI-native firms like data specialist Quantium? Gorton said Deloitte would return to growth this year after consecutive years of declining revenue across the big four locally. Deloitte, the largest of the big four in Australia, generated $2.55 billion in revenue in 2024-25.
As part of its research, Deloitte's work with the University of Sydney sought to identify how many tasks carried out by the hundreds of different advisory roles at the firm would be automated by AI. "It does vary depending on what the offering is, but ... up to about 30 per cent of tasks could be done by technology over the next three years," Gorton said.
The research showed that while each advisory role had a number of automatable tasks, none could be completely replaced by AI.
Deloitte's next step was to work out how to redeploy that 30 per cent of capacity instead of cutting staff numbers, which is how it came to focus on tripling its managed services arm.
Gorton said Deloitte's new strategy would help it generate revenue at a faster pace while only modestly growing its 11,000-strong workforce.
As part of its new strategy development, Deloitte also spoke to clients. "We wanted to start from an outside-in perspective: What fundamentally do you think you will need over the next five-plus years from us?" Gorton said.
The feedback was clients wanted Deloitte to share in their risks, rewards and have a stake in the outcome. "It's a real shift away from, 'I bill you for a service and then I walk away'. It's really about having true skin in the game."
This concept is most evident in Deloitte's renewed focus on running client back-office functions with the help of its Indian services centre.
"We currently have about $300 million of our total revenue sitting in this managed services bucket, where we run all areas such as finance and the technology, for clients," she said. "Finance is where we've probably got the strongest skill set."
The firm's advisers often find that back-office functions they take over have "a lot of manual processes", said Gorton. "They're often using Excel for way more than you could possibly ever imagine."
She said clients expect Deloitte to be able to do those functions for up to 40 per cent less.
"They will sit there and say, 'I want you to be able to bring that cost down by 30 to 40 per cent," she said. "Then you talk about, well, how much risk and reward is in there? How much are they going to wear? How much are we going to wear? And that's where this concept of partnering is really important, where we split the benefits."
summary: Deloitte Australia aims to expand its managed services arm to $1 billion by 2030, leveraging AI to enhance efficiency and reduce costs for clients amidst the threat of automation in consulting tasks.

heading: Data Science and AI
key_points: Deloitte plans to triple its managed services revenue by 2030.
AI is projected to automate 30% of routine consulting tasks within three years.
The firm is shifting to value pricing and absorbing operational risks for clients.
relevance: This development highlights the evolving landscape of consulting services, which is crucial for actuaries and financial professionals to understand as they adapt to technological advancements and changing client expectations.
